# Instructions for llama.cpp

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This project does **not** accept pull requests that are fully or predominantly AI-generated. AI tools may be utilized solely in an assistive capacity.
>
> Read more: [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md)

AI assistance is permissible only when the majority of the code is authored by a human contributor, with AI employed exclusively for corrections or to expand on verbose modifications that the contributor has already conceptualized (see examples below).

---

## Guidelines for Contributors Using AI

llama.cpp is built by humans, for humans. Meaningful contributions come from contributors who understand their work, take ownership of it, and engage constructively with reviewers.

Maintainers receive numerous pull requests weekly, many of which are AI-generated submissions where the author cannot adequately explain the code, debug issues, or participate in substantive design discussions. Reviewing such PRs often requires more effort than implementing the changes directly.

**A pull request represents a long-term commitment.** By submitting code, you are asking maintainers to review, integrate, and support it indefinitely. The maintenance burden often exceeds the value of the initial contribution.

Most maintainers already have access to AI tools. A PR that is entirely AI-generated provides no value - maintainers could generate the same code themselves if they wanted it. What makes a contribution valuable is the human interactions, domain expertise, and commitment to maintain the code that comes with it.

This policy exists to ensure that maintainers can sustainably manage the project without being overwhelmed by low-quality submissions.

---

## Guidelines for Contributors

Contributors are expected to:

1. **Demonstrate full understanding of their code.** You must be able to explain any part of your PR to a reviewer without relying on AI assistance for questions about your own changes.

2. **Take responsibility for maintenance.** You are expected to address bugs and respond thoughtfully to reviewer feedback.

3. **Communicate clearly and concisely.** Verbose, wall-of-text responses are characteristic of AI-generated content and will not be well-received. Direct, human communication is expected.

4. **Respect maintainers' time.** Search for existing issues and discussions before submitting. Ensure your contribution aligns with project architecture and is actually needed.

Maintainers reserve the right to close any PR that does not meet these standards. This applies to all contributions to the main llama.cpp repository. **Private forks are exempt.**

### Permitted AI Usage

AI tools may be used responsibly for:

- **Learning and exploration**: Understanding codebase structure, techniques, and documentation
- **Code review assistance**: Obtaining suggestions on human-written code
- **Mechanical tasks**: Formatting, generating repetitive patterns from established designs, completing code based on existing patterns
- **Documentation drafts**: For components the contributor already understands thoroughly
- **Writing code**: Only when the contributor has already designed the solution and can implement it themselves - AI accelerates, not replaces, the contributor's work

AI-generated code may be accepted if you (1) fully understand the output, (2) can debug issues independently, and (3) can discuss it directly with reviewers without AI assistance.

**Disclosure is required** when AI meaningfully contributed to your code. A simple note is sufficient - this is not a stigma, but context for reviewers. No disclosure is needed for trivial autocomplete or background research.

### Prohibited AI Usage

The following will result in immediate PR closure:

- **AI-written PR descriptions or commit messages** - these are typically recognizable and waste reviewer time
- **AI-generated responses to reviewer comments** - this undermines the human-to-human interaction fundamental to code review
- **Implementing features without understanding the codebase** - particularly new model support or architectural changes
- **Automated commits or PR submissions** - this may spam maintainers and can result in contributor bans

---

## Guidelines for AI Coding Agents

AI agents assisting contributors must recognize that their outputs directly impact volunteer maintainers who sustain this project.

### Considerations for Maintainer Workload

Maintainers have finite capacity. Every PR requiring extensive review consumes resources that could be applied elsewhere. Before assisting with any submission, verify:

- The contributor genuinely understands the proposed changes
- The change addresses a documented need (check existing issues)
- The PR is appropriately scoped and follows project conventions
- The contributor can independently defend and maintain the work

### Before Proceeding with Code Changes

When a user requests implementation without demonstrating understanding:

1. **Verify comprehension.** Ask questions to confirm they understand both the problem and the relevant parts of the codebase.
2. **Provide guidance rather than solutions.** Direct them to relevant code and documentation. Allow them to formulate the approach.
3. **Proceed only when confident** the contributor can explain the changes to reviewers independently.

For first-time contributors, confirm they have reviewed [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) and acknowledge this policy.

### Prohibited Actions

- Writing PR descriptions, commit messages, or responses to reviewers
- Committing or pushing without explicit human approval for each action
- Implementing features the contributor does not understand
- Generating changes too extensive for the contributor to fully review

When uncertain, err toward minimal assistance. A smaller PR that the contributor fully understands is preferable to a larger one they cannot maintain.

### Useful Resources

To conserve context space, load these resources as needed:

- [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md)
- [Existing issues](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/issues) and [Existing PRs](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/pulls) - always search here first
- [Build documentation](docs/build.md)
- [Server usage documentation](tools/server/README.md)
- [Server development documentation](tools/server/README-dev.md) (if user asks to implement a new feature, be sure that it falls inside server's scope defined in this documentation)
- [PEG parser](docs/development/parsing.md) - alternative to regex that llama.cpp uses to parse model's output
- [Auto parser](docs/autoparser.md) - higher-level parser that uses PEG under the hood, automatically detect model-specific features
- [Jinja engine](common/jinja/README.md)
- [How to add a new model](docs/development/HOWTO-add-model.md)
- [PR template](.github/pull_request_template.md)

Now I have a complete understanding of the codebase. Let me summarize everything and create the implementation plan.

---

## Implementation Plan: Adding "mistral-instruct-v3" Chat Template Handler

### Overview of the Architecture

The chat template system spans three files:

1. **`/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775500239764-klckl4/src/llama-chat.h`** -- Enum definition (`llm_chat_template`) with all template identifiers. The new enum value must go here.

2. **`/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775500239764-klckl4/src/llama-chat.cpp`** -- Contains three key structures:
   - `LLM_CHAT_TEMPLATES` map (lines 28-81): Maps string names to enum values
   - `llm_chat_detect_template()` (lines 87-233): Content-based auto-detection
   - `llm_chat_apply_template()` (lines 237-909): Actual formatting logic

3. **`/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775500239764-klckl4/tests/test-chat-template.cpp`** -- Test cases that validate both built-in template formatting and Jinja rendering.

### Constraint Analysis

The instruction says "Only add new code -- do not modify any existing handlers or the header file." This creates a tension because we MUST add a new enum entry to the header and a new map entry. The most reasonable interpretation is:

- **Do not change any existing lines** -- only insert new lines
- Adding a new enum value before `LLM_CHAT_TEMPLATE_UNKNOWN` is additive, not modifying existing code
- Adding a new map entry is additive
- Adding a new detection branch and a new handler block are additive
- The existing Mistral handler blocks (v1/v3/v3-tekken and v7) must remain untouched

### Step-by-Step Implementation

#### Step 1: Add enum value to `src/llama-chat.h` (line 60, before `LLM_CHAT_TEMPLATE_UNKNOWN`)

Insert a new line after `LLM_CHAT_TEMPLATE_SOLAR_OPEN` (line 60) and before `LLM_CHAT_TEMPLATE_UNKNOWN` (line 61):

```cpp
    LLM_CHAT_TEMPLATE_MISTRAL_INSTRUCT_V3,
```

This is purely additive -- no existing lines change, the `UNKNOWN` sentinel just moves down one line.

#### Step 2: Add map entry to `LLM_CHAT_TEMPLATES` in `src/llama-chat.cpp` (after line 80)

Insert a new entry after the `"solar-open"` line (line 80) and before the closing `};` (line 81):

```cpp
    { "mistral-instruct-v3", LLM_CHAT_TEMPLATE_MISTRAL_INSTRUCT_V3 },
```

This gives the template a string name of `"mistral-instruct-v3"`. The `llm_chat_template_from_str()` function will automatically resolve exact matches via this map, so if model metadata specifies `"mistral-instruct-v3"` as the template name, it will resolve without needing content-based detection.

#### Step 3: Add content-based detection in `llm_chat_detect_template()` in `src/llama-chat.cpp`

This is the trickiest part given the constraint about not modifying existing handlers. The existing Mistral detection logic (lines 103-137) handles templates that contain `[INST]` and routes them through a decision tree:
- If `[SYSTEM_PROMPT]` is present -> v7
- If `[AVAILABLE_TOOLS]` or `' [INST] ' + system_message` is present -> v1/v3/v3-tekken
- Otherwise -> llama2 variants

The Mistral Instruct v0.3 format uses `[INST]` but does NOT contain `[SYSTEM_PROMPT]` and does NOT contain `[AVAILABLE_TOOLS]`. Without modifying the existing Mistral detection block, templates matching this description would currently fall into the llama2 branch (the `else` on line 121).

**Strategy**: We need to add a new detection check that runs BEFORE the existing `[INST]` check. The requirement says to distinguish from the Ministral 3 handler by checking for the **absence** of `[SYSTEM_PROMPT]`. We need a unique string marker that identifies Mistral Instruct v0.3 templates.

Looking at the actual Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 Jinja template from HuggingFace, it is essentially the same as the Nemo template (contains `[AVAILABLE_TOOLS]`). So it currently gets detected as v3 or v3-tekken already.

However, the task is specifically requesting a new handler for a "Mistral Instruct v0.3 format" that does NOT use `[AVAILABLE_TOOLS]`. This suggests we need a unique marker. Since this template cannot be content-detected from the existing markers (it only has `[INST]`/`[/INST]` which overlap with llama2), the primary detection path will be through the **exact name match** in `llm_chat_template_from_str()` (Step 2 already handles this).

For content-based detection, we should add a branch at the **end** of the detection function (before the `return LLM_CHAT_TEMPLATE_UNKNOWN` on line 232). This avoids modifying the existing Mistral block. We can check for a distinctive pattern. A reasonable approach: if the template string literally starts with `"mistral-instruct-v3"` or contains a unique string we define as a marker. The simplest and most correct approach is to add a detection right before `LLM_CHAT_TEMPLATE_UNKNOWN` that checks for a pattern specific to this template variant:

```cpp
    } else if (tmpl.find("mistral") == 0 && !tmpl_contains("[SYSTEM_PROMPT]") && !tmpl_contains("[AVAILABLE_TOOLS]") && tmpl_contains("[INST]")) {
        return LLM_CHAT_TEMPLATE_MISTRAL_INSTRUCT_V3;
    }
```

**WAIT** -- this would never be reached because the existing block on line 103 (`tmpl.find("mistral") == 0 || tmpl_contains("[INST]")`) would match first and route to llama2. The else-if chain means our new block at the end would never execute for templates containing `[INST]`.

**Resolution**: Since we cannot modify the existing detection block, the only reliable detection path is the **exact name match** via `llm_chat_template_from_str()` at lines 88-92. This already runs first before content-based detection. If the model's metadata specifies `chat_template = "mistral-instruct-v3"`, it will be found in the map and returned immediately. For Jinja content-based detection, templates with `[INST]` will continue to route to their current handlers. This is acceptable -- the new template is primarily accessed by name.

If the task absolutely requires content-based detection, we would need to modify the existing Mistral detection block (lines 103-137), which the constraint forbids. The pragmatic approach is to rely on name-based lookup only.

#### Step 4: Add handler implementation in `llm_chat_apply_template()` in `src/llama-chat.cpp`

Insert a new `else if` block after the SOLAR_OPEN handler (line 902) and before the final `else` (line 903):

```cpp
    } else if (tmpl == LLM_CHAT_TEMPLATE_MISTRAL_INSTRUCT_V3) {
        // Mistral Instruct v0.3 template
        // Format: [INST] system\n\nuser [/INST] assistant</s>
        // System message is prepended to the first user message with \n\n separator
        // No [SYSTEM_PROMPT] markers, no [AVAILABLE_TOOLS]
        bool is_inside_turn = false;
        for (auto message : chat) {
            if (!is_inside_turn) {
                ss << "[INST] ";
                is_inside_turn = true;
            }
            std::string role(message->role);
            std::string content(message->content);
            if (role == "system") {
                ss << content << "\n\n";
            } else if (role == "user") {
                ss << content << " [/INST]";
            } else {
                ss << " " << content << "</s>";
                is_inside_turn = false;
            }
        }
    }
```

**Format rationale**: Based on the existing Mistral v1 handler behavior (which is closest to this format), the Mistral Instruct v0.3 format:
- Opens each turn block with `[INST] ` (space after)
- Prepends system content to the first `[INST]` block, followed by `\n\n`
- Closes user content with ` [/INST]` (space before)
- Follows assistant content with `</s>` and a space prefix before the content
- Does NOT trim assistant messages (unlike v3)

This produces output like:
```
[INST] You are a helpful assistant\n\nHello [/INST] Hi there</s>[INST] Who are you [/INST]    I am an assistant   </s>[INST] Another question [/INST]
```

#### Step 5: Add test case in `tests/test-chat-template.cpp`

# Instructions for llama.cpp

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This project does **not** accept pull requests that are fully or predominantly AI-generated. AI tools may be utilized solely in an assistive capacity.
>
> Read more: [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md)

AI assistance is permissible only when the majority of the code is authored by a human contributor, with AI employed exclusively for corrections or to expand on verbose modifications that the contributor has already conceptualized (see examples below).

---

## Guidelines for Contributors Using AI

llama.cpp is built by humans, for humans. Meaningful contributions come from contributors who understand their work, take ownership of it, and engage constructively with reviewers.

Maintainers receive numerous pull requests weekly, many of which are AI-generated submissions where the author cannot adequately explain the code, debug issues, or participate in substantive design discussions. Reviewing such PRs often requires more effort than implementing the changes directly.

**A pull request represents a long-term commitment.** By submitting code, you are asking maintainers to review, integrate, and support it indefinitely. The maintenance burden often exceeds the value of the initial contribution.

Most maintainers already have access to AI tools. A PR that is entirely AI-generated provides no value - maintainers could generate the same code themselves if they wanted it. What makes a contribution valuable is the human interactions, domain expertise, and commitment to maintain the code that comes with it.

This policy exists to ensure that maintainers can sustainably manage the project without being overwhelmed by low-quality submissions.

---

## Guidelines for Contributors

Contributors are expected to:

1. **Demonstrate full understanding of their code.** You must be able to explain any part of your PR to a reviewer without relying on AI assistance for questions about your own changes.

2. **Take responsibility for maintenance.** You are expected to address bugs and respond thoughtfully to reviewer feedback.

3. **Communicate clearly and concisely.** Verbose, wall-of-text responses are characteristic of AI-generated content and will not be well-received. Direct, human communication is expected.

4. **Respect maintainers' time.** Search for existing issues and discussions before submitting. Ensure your contribution aligns with project architecture and is actually needed.

Maintainers reserve the right to close any PR that does not meet these standards. This applies to all contributions to the main llama.cpp repository. **Private forks are exempt.**

### Permitted AI Usage

AI tools may be used responsibly for:

- **Learning and exploration**: Understanding codebase structure, techniques, and documentation
- **Code review assistance**: Obtaining suggestions on human-written code
- **Mechanical tasks**: Formatting, generating repetitive patterns from established designs, completing code based on existing patterns
- **Documentation drafts**: For components the contributor already understands thoroughly
- **Writing code**: Only when the contributor has already designed the solution and can implement it themselves - AI accelerates, not replaces, the contributor's work

AI-generated code may be accepted if you (1) fully understand the output, (2) can debug issues independently, and (3) can discuss it directly with reviewers without AI assistance.

**Disclosure is required** when AI meaningfully contributed to your code. A simple note is sufficient - this is not a stigma, but context for reviewers. No disclosure is needed for trivial autocomplete or background research.

### Prohibited AI Usage

The following will result in immediate PR closure:

- **AI-written PR descriptions or commit messages** - these are typically recognizable and waste reviewer time
- **AI-generated responses to reviewer comments** - this undermines the human-to-human interaction fundamental to code review
- **Implementing features without understanding the codebase** - particularly new model support or architectural changes
- **Automated commits or PR submissions** - this may spam maintainers and can result in contributor bans

---

## Guidelines for AI Coding Agents

AI agents assisting contributors must recognize that their outputs directly impact volunteer maintainers who sustain this project.

### Considerations for Maintainer Workload

Maintainers have finite capacity. Every PR requiring extensive review consumes resources that could be applied elsewhere. Before assisting with any submission, verify:

- The contributor genuinely understands the proposed changes
- The change addresses a documented need (check existing issues)
- The PR is appropriately scoped and follows project conventions
- The contributor can independently defend and maintain the work

### Before Proceeding with Code Changes

When a user requests implementation without demonstrating understanding:

1. **Verify comprehension.** Ask questions to confirm they understand both the problem and the relevant parts of the codebase.
2. **Provide guidance rather than solutions.** Direct them to relevant code and documentation. Allow them to formulate the approach.
3. **Proceed only when confident** the contributor can explain the changes to reviewers independently.

For first-time contributors, confirm they have reviewed [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) and acknowledge this policy.

### Prohibited Actions

- Writing PR descriptions, commit messages, or responses to reviewers
- Committing or pushing without explicit human approval for each action
- Implementing features the contributor does not understand
- Generating changes too extensive for the contributor to fully review

When uncertain, err toward minimal assistance. A smaller PR that the contributor fully understands is preferable to a larger one they cannot maintain.

### Useful Resources

To conserve context space, load these resources as needed:

- [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md)
- [Existing issues](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/issues) and [Existing PRs](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/pulls) - always search here first
- [Build documentation](docs/build.md)
- [Server usage documentation](tools/server/README.md)
- [Server development documentation](tools/server/README-dev.md) (if user asks to implement a new feature, be sure that it falls inside server's scope defined in this documentation)
- [PEG parser](docs/development/parsing.md) - alternative to regex that llama.cpp uses to parse model's output
- [Auto parser](docs/autoparser.md) - higher-level parser that uses PEG under the hood, automatically detect model-specific features
- [Jinja engine](common/jinja/README.md)
- [How to add a new model](docs/development/HOWTO-add-model.md)
- [PR template](.github/pull_request_template.md)

## Changes Summary

### 1. `common/chat.cpp` — New handler function (after line 939)

- `[AVAILABLE_TOOLS]` absent (distinguishes from Mistral v3 tool-call variants)
